steps.title: Identify the boundary problem
steps.content:
The sentence reads: "...Ray released his first movie, Pather ______ quiet black-and-white drama about a family in rural India, Ray's film was quite different..." After the film title comes a descriptive phrase ("a quiet black-and-white drama..."), then a new independent clause ("Ray's film was quite different"). These need a proper sentence boundary, not a comma.
  • Choice A — Panchali a: No punctuation between the title and the next sentence — this fuses two sentences into a run-on. ✗
  • Choice B — Panchali, which was a: Adding "which was" turns the phrase into a relative clause but still creates a comma splice between "India" and "Ray's film was quite different." ✗
  • Choice C — Panchali, a: A comma alone cannot separate two independent clauses; this is a comma splice. ✗
  • Choice D — Panchali. A: A period correctly closes the first sentence at the title. The descriptive phrase "A quiet black-and-white drama..." then opens a new sentence modifying "Ray's film." ✓

steps.title: Confirm the match
steps.content: Only a period creates a clean boundary between the two sentences without producing a comma splice or run-on. That matches Choice D — Panchali. A.
